name: What is the difference between floating, glue-down, and nail-down flooring?

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between floating, glue-down, and nail-down flooring installation methods?
mainEntity.text: What is the difference between floating, glue-down, and nail-down flooring installation methods?

acceptedAnswer.text: The installation method affects how the floor performs, how much prep is needed, and where it can be used. Floating floors connect to each other and sit over the subfloor, often with underlayment beneath them. They are faster to install and can be a good choice when you want less demolition or easier replacement. Glue-down floors are attached directly to the subfloor with adhesive, which can create a more solid feel but requires careful moisture control and surface prep. Nail-down floors are secured with fasteners and are common with certain hardwood products over wood subfloors. Each method has tradeoffs. Floating systems can feel slightly less rigid. Glue-down systems need excellent prep but can reduce movement. Nail-down flooring depends heavily on the condition of the subfloor and framing. The best choice depends on the room, the existing subfloor, and the product manufacturer’s rules. Ask the installer which method is recommended for your home and why, rather than choosing by price alone.
acceptedAnswer.description: Floating floors lock together and rest over underlayment without being fixed to the subfloor. Glue-down floors are bonded to the subfloor, and nail-down floors are fastened with nails or staples, usually over wood subfloors. Each method has different noise, moisture, and prep requirements.
